title: Research
text:
I am interested in marine ecosystems, ecological change, and environmental data analysis.







Urchin Abundance Research Experiment


SBC LTER Experiment


Field-based ecological monitoring of sea urchin populations along the Santa Barbara coastline.




More details


This project is part of the Santa Barbara Coastal Long Term Ecological Research (SBC LTER) program. I work alongside team members to conduct biweekly experiments at multiple coastal sites. Data include larval settlement rates, dietary composition effects on growth and gonad development, fertilization success, and forage rate across long-term time series datasets.


 View Project PDF 







Coast Live Oak Gall Growth Experiment


Ecosystem Change Project


A 9-week field experiment analyzing gall formation and ecosystem response in Coast Live Oak trees.




More details


This class-based research project investigated how abiotic factors influence gall formation and grass growth. We tested environmental drivers of plant–insect interactions and quantified variation across sites in Isla Vista, CA using observational and experimental data collection.


 View Project PDF

title: Home
text:
I am an Environmental Studies student focused on marine and forest ecology, I plan on continuing my studies in Coastal Science and and Ecology. I spend my time enjoying many hobbies including painting, backpacking, surfing, and scuba diving.
I am passionate about understanding ocean ecosystems and working in an outdoor environment.

title: Dive Data Exploration
text:
Dive Depth Data Visualization

# create data set of dive depths 
depths <- c(
38,40,44,15,40,40,
50,40,44,35,32,32,60
)

summary(depths)

   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
  15.00   35.00   40.00   39.23   44.00   60.00 

# create visualization 
library(ggplot2)

dives <- data.frame(
  Dive = 1:length(depths),
  Depth = depths
)
# plot x and y axes, set labels and titles
ggplot(dives,
       aes(x = Dive,
           y = Depth)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(
    title = "Depth of Recorded Dives",
    x = "Dive Number",
    y = "Depth (ft)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()










Results
The average dive depth was approximately 39 feet. Most dives ranged between 32 and 50 feet, while the deepest dive reached 60 feet.
This exercise demonstrates how ecological and recreational field data can be explored using simple visualization techniques in R.
